title: Schwabacher, the German Gothic hand
subtitle: The rounded blackletter that preceded Fraktur in German printing
excerpt: Schwabacher is the rounded Gothic hand that dominated German printing from roughly 1470 to 1530, before Fraktur became the dominant German script. Its letterforms are fuller and rounder than Fraktur, less angular than Textura, and closer to the handwritten Gothic cursives of the period.
sourceNotes: Rudolf Koch, Das Schreiben als Kunstfertigkeit (published in early-20th-century editions predating 1928), contains structural analysis of Schwabacher letterforms consistent with pre-Fraktur German manuscript hands. The historical context draws on public-domain surveys of German typography in the incunabula period.

glossaryTerms.definition: A German Gothic blackletter style of the late 15th and early 16th centuries, characterised by rounded letterforms and diamond-shaped finials, midway between the angular Textura and the pointed Fraktur.
The lozenge-shaped terminal ornament at the top of an entry stroke and the base of a downstroke in Schwabacher and related Gothic hands.
In blackletter calligraphy, a secondary stroke that closes or completes the body of a letter, often an arching movement that returns to the baseline.

content.text: Schwabacher sits between the rigid angularity of Gothic Textura and the hairline entries of Fraktur. Its defining features are rounded bowls (the o, b, d, g, p, q are more oval than pointed),
at stroke heads and feet, and a
that closes many letterforms from below. The hand is written at approximately 45 degrees nib angle from the baseline.

items.name: Brause Bandzug nib
items.notes: The Bandzug's stiffness suits the firm pressure of Schwabacher well. A Mitchell Roundhand 2 or 3 is also workable.

items.name: Sumi ink
items.notes: Dense black for the highest contrast with the paper.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm
items.notes: Ruled at 5 nib-widths for the x-height, with ascender and descender zones at 3 nib-widths each.

content.text: Hold the Brause Bandzug at a constant 45 degrees from the baseline. Unlike Foundational (which rotates the nib for horizontal strokes) or Italic (which varies slightly between upstrokes and downstrokes),
keeps 45 degrees throughout. At this angle, the downstroke produces the maximum thick stroke and the horizontal hairline produces the maximum thin.

at the x-height or cap-height line. Place the full face of the nib on the paper at a slight leftward tilt, press gently to make a small lozenge shape, and then draw the stroke downward to the baseline. The lozenge is formed by a brief lateral rock of the nib before the downward pull. Practise making consistent lozenges before attempting letterforms.

The o in Schwabacher is a rounded oval, not the pointed lozenge of Textura Quadrata. The curve is made in two strokes: a left-curving downstroke from the x-height to the baseline, and a right-curving counterstroke that closes from below. The two strokes create a slightly tilted oval. Begin with the i (diamond head, full downstroke,
at foot), then n (i-body plus an arch), then o (two-stroke oval), before tackling the full alphabet.

items.symptom: The diamond finials are inconsistent in size and angle
items.cause: The initial nib placement varies from stroke to stroke.
items.fix: Practise the lozenge mark on its own until the hand makes it without effort. Then return to letterforms.

items.symptom: The o looks more like Textura than Schwabacher
items.cause: The curves are being pulled too sharply to points at head and foot.
items.fix: Slow the curved stroke and let the nib follow a fully rounded path. The two halves of the o should meet at a slight curve, not a sharp point.

items.symptom: The letters look too light and grey rather than dense and black
items.cause: The nib is not fully loaded, or the 45-degree angle has drifted lower.
items.fix: Dip more frequently and check the nib angle before each downstroke.
